data.title: The Hidden Engine Behind Zeta's Surge
data.description:
Zeta Global Holdings (ZETA) is quickly finding itself to be one of the most differentiated companies in the enterprise marketing technology stack. Zeta’s latest offering, Athena, marks a pivotal point in Zeta’s trajectory as a company, but also in relation to how AI is going to shape enterprise operations in general.

In my earlier analysis, the focus was on Zeta Global’s evolving ability to drive deeper, more efficient monetization from its existing customer base, powered by multi-use adoption, expanding ARPU, and AI-driven workflow automation, which positions the company for a substantial valuation re-rating through 2028.

The focus now is how, following the latest earnings, Athena is driving a powerful wave of operational leverage and margin expansion that positions Zeta for a meaningful valuation re-rating as it increasingly behaves like an AI-native infrastructure company rather than a traditional martech platform.

The AI as the New UI Thesis

There is an emerging debate in Zeta’s contention that AI as the new UI expresses an even more fundamental philosophy of organizations, which is that the future of differentiation is in intelligent interfaces, not merely intelligent algorithms. Unlike most of its competitors, Zeta’s AI features have, from day one in 2017, been designed into the platform, which provides Athena an immense structural advantage, having an intelligence that rests upon a deterministic data engine as opposed to approximations of probability.

Athena is Zeta’s AI engine that automates workflows, analyzes data in real time, and delivers personalized recommendations to improve marketing performance. In practice, it acts like an intelligent assistant built directly into the platform, helping customers do more while reducing manual effort and cost.

Zeta’s Data Cloud, which is behind Athena, covers more than 242 million deterministic US individuals, in addition to over 550 million international profiles, updated in real time for billions of signals. This results in accurate personalization, accurate attribution, and compliant activation, which current general AI models find difficult to do.

The outcome is an AI solution that is able to root all recommendations in trustworthy, first-party data. For companies operating in a scenario of increasing constraints in privacy regulation, as well as the impending end of third-party cookies, this is a sustainable competitive barrier.

Athena’s interface is very effective at democratizing access to such intelligence. Zeta’s solution makes complex orchestration accessible to people who aren’t tech-savvy, which enables organizations to add more without having to add complexity at scale.

Operational Leverage and Margin Expansion

The playing field for operating leverage could be greatly impacted by Athena’s addition to this sector. Zeta’s adjusted EBITDA margin grew by 320 basis points from the previous year in Q3 of 2025 to 23.2%, which is also the company’s 19th consecutive period of improving margins, thanks to integration benefits from AI-powered automation, which cuts down human labor in campaign management, thus allowing Zeta to grow revenue without correspondingly increasing costs.

As Athena stimulates more Feature usage, the unit cost of revenue growth simultaneously decreases, fostering a compounding profitability trend. The Company’s free cash flow margin is already at 14%, an 83% year-over-year improvement, although a target of 70% of adjusted EBITDA to Free Cash Flow is set for 2030.

Longer-term, Athena is also a productivity amplifier for the organization as a whole. Through the automation of sales enablement, data modeling, and customer support, it has the potential to decrease selling and administrative costs expressed as a percentage of revenue. The compounding of this benefit could drive margins into the mid to high 20% range by 2027, which would be competitive with peer SaaS companies.

Competitive Landscape Outpacing Legacy AI Implementations

The race between AI in marketing technology is gaining pace, but this is because not all contenders have an equal level of ground to run from. The two current industry leaders in enterprise marketing, namely Salesforce (CRM) and Adobe (ADBE), are essentially building upon an older infrastructure as they implement an additional layer of Generative AI Assistants in front of them.

The capabilities of Einstein Copilot by Salesforce, as well as Sensei GenAI from Adobe, in terms of content analysis as well as analytics, leave much to be desired, but they’re hobbled by infrastructure debt that’s several decades old, which impacts real-time capabilities.

Zeta, by contrast, is designed from scratch to incorporate AI integration, which makes it possible to natively integrate conversational intelligence capabilities during campaign execution. As for Zeta’s infrastructure, it supports native integration of conversational intelligence capabilities during campaign execution, which results in low latency, increased reliability, and closed-loop learning capabilities.

Furthermore, Zeta’s competitive advantage derives from focusing primarily on the mid-market and enterprise segment, without having to deal with the burden of implementing large projects in terms of costs. While an enterprise project in Salesforce could mean deploying in months or even years, costing millions of dollars, Zeta achieves an average deploy time of 50% sooner, along with an average ROI of 6:1, as shared during Zeta Live.

Valuation: A Re-Rating Story in Progress

Zeta’s valuation is becoming more dependent than ever upon the quality of its revenue runway, which provides a clear trajectory to significant scale. Analysts project revenue of $1.54 billion by 2026, and the guidance from management for 2028 of over $2.1 billion indicates strong revenue CAGR of an impressive 20%. At today’s levels, the stock trades at a forward P/S of roughly 3.2x for 2026 and just about 2x on 2028 estimates, multiples that appear unusually low for a company expanding this quickly while lifting margins.

The degree of visibility into Zeta’s future, combined with increasing margins and cash flow, makes current multiples appear increasingly disconnected from where the business is headed. As Zeta matures into a bigger, more optimized platform, it appears likely that so, too, will markets, creating significant upside as revenue compounds in the coming years.

Risks I Am Watching For

Even as Athena strengthens Zeta’s competitive advantages, certain risks to execution remain. The forthcoming combination of its enterprise software business, Marathon, into Marigold raises operational complexity inasmuch as product infrastructure and staff combine.

A slowdown in unlocking cross-sell benefits might slow growth’s trajectory for a period. There is also an adoptance risk because chat interfaces using AI remain in an early phase of an enterprise’s S-Curve, as well as Zeta’s potential exposure to payment cycles of an agency, which may lead to quarterly volatility in free cash flow even in strong fundamentals.

However, these risks appear to be more related to execution than structural problems, and having a strong balance sheet, stable revenue, as well as established margins, provides distinct protection against downside risk.

Final Take

Athena shows how Zeta Global is positioned as a rare AI-native platform that has real potential to reshape and redefine the infrastructure of enterprise marketing. As it refines the usage experience from code to conversation, it does more than merely drive customer engagement but also forges ways to tap into revenue streams.

As adoption continues to expand, and the market begins to understand compounding economics, Zeta’s valuation discount appears increasingly unsustainable. For those investors looking for differentiated exposure to the nexus of AI, Data, and Enterprise SaaS, Athena may prove to be what catalyzes Zeta’s potential into actualized market dominance.

data.title: RAPT Therapeutics: Strong Promise In Immunology, Looking Like An Opportunity At These Prices
data.description:
Topline Summary

RAPT Therapeutics (RAPT) is a company I've taken one look at before, several years ago, coming to the conclusion that this immunology-focused biotech had generated market value far, far beyond their data at the time. Since then, the share price has fallen over 80% (although the total valuation of the company has dropped more modestly, from around $850 million in 2023 to more like $450 million at the time of writing). So the company has absolutely come down to earth, and they've had much more time to mature their pipeline. Today, I want to make a case for a tentative "Buy" thesis, and one that could pay off if you navigate the risks carefully.

Pipeline Overview - Immunology Focus With Broad Implications

RAPT has built its pipeline on two prongs: agents that block immunoglobulin E (IgE), and agents that block CCR4, both of which are implicated in inflammation, making it a potentially suitable approach in rheumatology and cancer therapy.

Ozurpreprubart (RPT904)

The most advanced candidate in the RAPT pipeline is ozurpreprubart, an antibody blocking IgE. This immunoglobulin is a principal player in inflammatory reactions generated by allergens like peanuts. Ozurpreprubart is currently in mid-stage clinical study for a few indications, most notably chronic spontaneous urticaria (aka hives).

This indication was the subject of a few key catalysts for RAPT in recent months. Back in October, the company announced a positive topline data readout for ozurpreprubart from a phase 2 trial. The study took 137 patients with urticaria not well controlled with antihistamines and randomized them to either ozurpreprubart or the approved agent omalizumab. Patients receiving ozurpreprubart had a numeric improvement in the urticaria activity score over omalizumab, and this improvement (or at least no clear sign of decrement) was achieved with dosing every 8 weeks or every 12 weeks, compared with the every-4-week dosing of omalizumab.

These findings point to ozurpreprubart being a more favorable option, given the longer spans between doses and favorable tolerability. RAPT and their Chinese partner anticipate advancing this compound to a phase 3 trial, although the exact timeline for this study to begin has not been shared.

Another important area being explored for ozurpreprubart is food allergy. RAPT has recently initiated a randomized, phase 2 trial evaluating the agent compared with placebo in ~100 people with at least one food allergy, with a primary endpoint of a prespecified target improvement in a controlled, oral food challenge.

Tivumecirnon

Tivumecirnon is an oral molecule blocking CCR4, opening potential avenues for T-cell lymphoma and other forms of cancer. This agent was actually one of the main areas of focus in my first write-up on the company a few years ago. However, not much other progress has been completed in favor of pursuing ozurpreprubart with all force. As such, to date only a pair of early trials have been completed, and the agent no longer features prominently in their corporate presentation. So for now it would appear as though tivumecirnon is on the back burner.

Financial Overview

At the end of Q3 2025, RAPT held $37.9 million in cash and equivalents, as well as $119.4 million in marketable securities. Operating expenses were $19.4 million, and after other income, the net loss for the quarter was $17.6 million. For the first 9 months of 2025, the cash burn was $76.1 million. At this rate, RAPT has a cash runway of approximately 6 quarters. However, these figures do not take into account proceeds from a public offering of common stock that RAPT expects to bring in a gross of $250 million.

This could add several more years of operational runway for RAPT to continue their pivotal trial developments, bringing the cash runway to around 16 quarters, with the assumption that costs remain the same.

Strengths and Risks

After years of work, RAPT appears poised to generate pivotal data that could lead to approval in urticaria, a space where Xolair (omalizumab mentioned above) currently commands global sales in the multiple billions. But there are some issues to consider.

Strength - Favorable randomized phase 2 data

While their trial was designed neither as a superiority nor a non-inferiority study, ozurpreprubart has shown a strong signal that it could at least replace Xolair with less frequent dosing. The randomized, phase 3 trial could end up showing that ozurpreprubart is, in fact, better than omalizumab, with the potential to capture a large portion of the market.

Risk - Not the Only Game in town

There are several treatment approaches to chronic spontaneous urticaria. The most recent of these is remibrutinib, an oral inhibitor of BTK approved in patients who are symptomatic despite treatment with antihistamines. And that drug passed the phase 3 bar in two separate trials published in the New England Journal. This ignores that omalizumab and dupilumab are both available, as well, and have been for years.

All of this is to say that ozurpreprubart faces competition even if it does get approval, and we do not yet know that they will be able to differentiate this compound from competitors adequately. In particular, patients may elect for the BTK inhibitor more often, since it is orally dosed.

Risk - Cash burn will accelerate and may place pressure on RAPT at an inconvenient time

Over the years, RAPT has remained fairly resilient in total market valuation despite dilution that has led to a steep decline in share price. This latest round of common stock offering shows what a developmental biotech can do from a position of strength. An informal calculation suggests they now have something like 16 quarters of cash to continue their projects (taking into account their recent public offering), but it is important to keep in mind that a large, phase 3 trial will incur extra cash burn, and new forays into food allergy will also elevate the operational outlay.

If they're unable to continue keeping costs under control, then RAPT may find themselves in need of another dilutive financing before their phase 3 trial reads out.

Bottom-Line Summary

RAPT has definitely come a long way since my first outing with the company. And I have very much evolved my position in favor of the thesis, especially now that the company is trading at only a small premium relative to their cash after they complete their public offering.

So to me it is clearly no longer a "Sell" for me, but the question is whether these data are strong enough to justify a "Buy." And given the market size, I think ozurpreprubart has the potential to demonstrate a niche, and the phase 3 trial may well end up establishing this agent as a standard of care.

So for now I am upgrading my sentiment to "Buy," given the promise of their pipeline, the market size they're targeting (with currently approved drugs commanding billions in global sales per year), and the strong signals of efficacy relative to omalizumab. In particular, the fact that the market now values this pipeline in the tens of millions of dollars suggests that these price levels represent a viable entry point. I think RAPT is definitely worth watching closely for further development, or potentially a small position for people willing to take the risk of a phase 3 trial that will take a while to develop.

data.title: The 3% World: The Bond Market Tells What The FED Won't Say (But Markets Don't Care)
data.description:
A New "Tolerated" Regime

The world has changed.

Inflation, unemployment rates, Treasury yields, and interest rates are all in different ranges from where we were used to seeing them. We initially thought that this could be the long-tail effect of the pandemic. By now, we can probably argue that the macroeconomic paradigm has changed.

In this article, I want to explain what is happening, showing the impact that it can have on our investing strategies and the way we build our portfolios.

Macroeconomic Reasons That Make 2% Inflation Impossible

The ZIRP era is behind us, and inflation won't be at 2% for some time. There are structural cost pressures that can't be simply wiped away. The first one is linked to tariffs. The Yale Budget Lab, for example, calculates that in the short run, tariffs imply a 1.7% increase in consumer prices. But even without these levies, we live in a world where wage growth is still stronger than before the pandemic. Zero Interest Rate Policy belongs, as of now, to economic history handbooks and describes the period from 2009 to 2021. That's when money was basically free, and FED fund rates were mainly between 0% and 0.25%. Those who signed a mortgage back then are now feasting and have no hurry whatsoever in paying it down. Inflation is melting the value of their debt down, while wages are increasing at 3.6%.

One of the drivers of this increase in labor cost is the aging workforce, which naturally shifts workers toward higher-paying jobs. Participation in the labor market is also below pre-pandemic levels. Supply chain constraints keep appearing, creating both labor scarcity and harder-to-achieve productivity gains. Simply put, the cost structure is overall increasing. We then need to consider that China is not viewed anymore as the cheap factory of the world and that the U.S. is pursuing a re-industrialization policy focused on reshoring or nearshoring most of the manufacturing capacity it needs. This implies higher input costs.

Third, massive infrastructure spending driven by the AI buildout is pouring billions into the economy, which is, per se, an inflationary force at work (the article "Capex Nation" that I wrote deals with this in depth).

Therefore, inflation is now precisely at 3%. Even though we know that the FED's mandate targets 2% inflation over the long term, many facts signal that the FED is probably accepting 3% inflation for some time. It has not been explicitly said, but its behavior suggests that 3% inflation can be tolerated, at least as long as it doesn't accelerate. After all, accepting 3% right now still means that YoY inflation has cooled. In fact, if the FED really wanted inflation to be at 2% quickly, it could have tightened its rate policy this summer, when inflation trended upward from 2.8% to 3.1%. Instead, it first paused hikes and then cut rates, also signaling that, perhaps, more cuts could be down the line. The economic cost of pushing inflation rapidly to 2% is high; it would imply more unemployment and lower wage growth. But, right now, with inflation somewhat tamed, though at a higher level than the FED's mandate targets, the FED has pivoted to prioritizing the labor market since the unemployment rate has reached 4.5%.

As a consequence, Treasuries keep yielding above 4%, and this will last longer than expected. I think this is also an indicator that signals that investors are expecting 3% inflation for some time. The result is that the curve of Treasury yields has to shift higher. Yields don't know anything about the FED's mandate; they simply reflect what investors want as real returns. With inflation at 3%, a 4% risk-free return is the minimum standard that is required, calculated as the sum of expected inflation and the premium investors want. The bond market has been trading at these levels since 2022, with only a short period of time between 2022 and 2023 where yields dropped to around 3.5%. High inflation could help the government reduce the real value of its debt. But, at the same time, it creates a situation where it has to pay back this advantage in the form of higher interest rates. So, the trade-off is a short-run real debt erosion in exchange for paying higher interest in the long run.

What does this mean? Well, a 3% world is—structurally - structurally speaking - a higher-yield world.

Consequences In A 3% World

If we use the BLS historical CPI from 1913 to 2024, we see that the average inflation in the 80s was 5.1% and that in the 90s it was around 3%. Then, in the first decade of this century, we see it move down to 2.6% and in the 10s it averaged 1.8%. So, inflation had been falling for over 40 years until 2021. We don't know if history will repeat, but it could take up to a decade or even fifteen years for the average inflation to be clearly down below 2%. Right now, this decade has averaged around 4%.

Now, let's combine this with the average 10-year Treasury yield. Let's look at what happened in the past 5 decades. In the 80s, the average yield was above 10%. In the 90s, it retraced to 6.7%. In the early 2000s, the 10-yr average yield was 4.5%. Then, in the 10s, the yield dropped to 2.4$. At the end of the ZIRP era, it was even down to a minuscule 1.2%. Since the post-pandemic rebound, the yield has moved up quite a bit and is now between 3.8% and 4.5%. For more than three consecutive years, the 10-year Treasury yield has consistently been above 3.5%. So, the downtrend is broken, and the yield curve is showing a structural change that reflects a true repricing. Where is the underlying fundamental reason? Inflation. Or more precisely, the premium over inflation that investors demand.

Now let's combine these data with what the New York Fed points out about the real way yields are formed:

In standard economic theory, yields on Treasury securities are composed of two components: expectations of the future path of short-term Treasury yields and the Treasury term premium. The term premium is defined as the compensation that investors require for bearing the risk that interest rates may change over the life of the bond.

In the 2010s, expectations of future rates were close to 0 while the term premium was also kept very low by QE and extremely low inflation. Today, the market expects higher short-term rates, while the term premium is once again building up. The market simply wants to be more protected against higher inflation (or, at least, against inflation risk) and rate volatility.

This chart below shows exactly how the 10-year Treasury yield is made up of two components: in blue we see the 10-year yield; in red we see the term premium, while the yellow line represents the fitted yield, that is, what the yield should be when we add short-term expectations and the term premium. Starting from the 1980s, we see three important things:

  1. The downtrend from the early 80s to 2020 (from 15% to just 1%). It shows what many have called the long bond bull market.
  2. The term premium fell from 3-4% into negative territory, where it stayed from 2015 until 2021. This means that investors were ok to accept yields below the expected short-rate yield for holding long-term bonds. This was possible only because inflation was extremely low, and QE de facto removed duration from the market.
  3. After 2021, the regime changed, with higher 10-year yields and a positive term premium, showing that both components of how Treasury yields are formed have moved and are now higher.

Now, interest rates averaged -0.05% from 2011 to 2020. Now, we are seeing rates with positive real yields. This also represents a shift, or, as some believe, simply returning to normality. With inflation allowed to stay around 3%, the FED has more room between the neutral stance (interest rates around 3%) and 0%. Which means the so-called dovish area widens. But the chart above shows that the 10-year yield is not higher now just because interest rates are higher, but also because investors demand higher compensation due to a more uncertain outlook.

Investing Implications

First of all, long-duration bonds should now be underweighted. Short-term Treasuries have a better risk/reward ratio in an environment where inflation is stickier than expected, and at 3%.

Secondly, and perhaps the most important thing to say, discount rates now have to be higher, which leads to lower multiples. Equity risk premia should rise, and, logically speaking, we should expect that market multiples in a 3% inflation regime should be lower compared to those of the last decade.

Third, what kinds of businesses thrive in this environment? Well, companies that can pass through inflation rather than suffer extreme pressure on their margins will probably fare better than many others. Consumer discretionary stocks, but also levered REITS, could be some of the losers, while commodity and energy plays could act as natural hedges.

This leads us to a paradox, or at least a contradiction, in today's market. The macro case we have built shows that structurally higher inflation, rates, yields, and term premia lead to lower valuation multiples. But the market right now is doing exactly the opposite, since valuations are expanding, especially when we consider AI-related names. Is this a bubble? Well, not really. In fact, while many companies (look at the hyperscalers) do trade at higher multiples compared to the 2010s, their earnings growth is, generally speaking, accelerating or, at least, maintaining a brisk pace. This happens even if the cost of capital is now higher.

The AI capex cycle, as we said earlier, is inflationary. For example, where data centers are built, electricity prices surge. But AI capex is an investment that is also producing earnings growth. When companies grow faster than their cost of capital, the market usually bids their prices up. So, multiples right now are supported by sustained earnings growth. Actually, we could even say that AI is so big that its effect currently dominates over the other structural trends that should lead to multiple compressions. It also explains why the market is currently pushed higher by a handful of companies, while most of the companies in the S&P 500 are not compounding as fast.

We often hear people saying that the market is only fueled by the Mag 7 and that the S&P 493 is hardly doing anything. Well, perhaps we now see why. The market should see multiple compressions right now. But a big megatrend has started that is producing earnings growth for some companies, especially the hyperscalers and the few other companies that are positioned at key bottlenecks of the supply chain. Right now, the Mag-7 represents almost 36% of the index. In 2023, the market rose 26.3%, with the Mag 7 contributing with 15.8%, an extremely large chunk of the market's total returns. In 2024, the market gained another 25.7%, with the Mag 7 contributing with 14.1%. So far, the market is up 16% and the Mag 7 account for over half of these returns. Perhaps the market is not behaving in an irrational way, but we have a hard time identifying the correct way to interpret what is happening.

Conclusion

Indeed, the landscape has changed, and we can't just expect to go back to the 2010s. After all, even as investors, we are probably sitting on huge gains with our portfolios at ATHs. I mean, we can be well-off, even in this macroeconomic environment. Inflation seems to be settled around 3% and the bond market is seeing a structural repricing process. In this context, long-duration assets lose steam because discount rates go naturally higher, lowering valuation multiples. But, at the same time, the AI capex boom is reshaping the market, producing strong earnings growth for a selected number of companies, whose strong performance is so meaningful that it is able to drive up the whole market, pushing the S&P 500 to richer multiples, even if Treasuries yield 4%.

The playbook for investors seems to me clear. Shorter maturities will be favored. We will use higher discount rates in our valuation models, and we will favor companies with strong pricing power (ever heard of Ferrari?), high ROIC, or direct and immediately understandable exposure to AI. Sectors that benefit from inflation will also be rich in opportunities, while others will probably suffer greatly from higher inflation.

A 3% world is not a low-rate paradise, but it is also not a bad world for investors. We simply have to pick stocks having in mind a new mindset.

data.title: Royalty Pharma: Guidance Raised Again, Platform Strength Intact -- Buy Reiterated
data.description:
Following Royalty Pharma plc's (NASDAQ:RPRX) Investor Day (Fig. 1), we are returning with an update in light of the company's supportive Q3 results and its latest investment. Here at the Lab, we reiterate a Buy rating on the back of solid execution and a fresh upward revision to guidance. The company still trades at a notable discount to biopharma peers, despite 86% of the portfolio being tied to approved therapies and free cash flow expanding at a healthy clip. With the Investor Day, Royalty Pharma management targets 11–14% portfolio receipts CAGR through 2030, and continues to deploy capital with discipline, including selective M&A.

Fig 1

Q3 Results and Our Positive Stance

In the quarter, RP's portfolio receipts grew by 11% to $814 million (Fig. 2), exceeding the average consensus expectation of $765 million. The portfolio increased mainly thanks to Voranigo, Tremfya, and the cystic fibrosis franchise. On the cash flow statement, the company reported operating activities of $703 million. For Q3, RP ROIC and ROE reached 15.7% and 22.9%, respectively (Fig. 3). Regarding capital allocation, RP deployed approximately $1 billion in the quarter, driven by upfront payments under the Imdelltra and Obexelimab funding agreements. YTD, the deployment reached $1.7 billion. At the same time, aside from a dividend payment, the company has repurchased approximately 4 million shares in Q3, and $1.2 billion in 9M. Once again, RP raised its full-year 2025 outlook with "Portfolio Receipts expected to be $3,200 to $3,250 million."

Source: Royalty Pharma Q3 results - Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we still positive?

Even after a strong +55% YTD performance, the company still sits about 20% below the following days' IPO offerings, underscoring fragile market sentiment. Here at the Lab, we have a contrarian view here. This is backed by the fact that the company raised the guidance three times this year, Q1 (May 8) - Q2 (Aug 6), and following the Q3 results. Additionally, this new guidance still factors in conservative assumptions regarding cystic fibrosis franchise royalties

More importantly, the biotech market is still heavily disrupted. The company offers generalist investors a diversified route into biopharma without the single-drug binary risk typical of the sector. Here at the Lab, we believe the market has not fully understood RP's potential. This is a company that functions as a platform-style aggregator. The key to the report is the fact that pharma R&D costs are rising and capital needs are expanding. The company's business is royalty financing, and it is gaining traction as a non-dilutive complement to equity and debt. Additionally, the company commands roughly half of the royalty market, reinforcing its central role as the asset class and its firepower to pursue bigger deals.

There is a strong asset-quality mix. Its portfolio focuses on royalties from scale franchises with high commercial potential therapies. However, the company recently announced a royalty financing deal for Alnylam’s Amvuttra (Fig. 4). The company projects nominal peak sales for Amvuttra of almost $11 billion at a 1% royalty rate. Having said that, there is also pipeline portfolio progress ahead, which includes six Ph3 readouts over the next year. We see these near-term readouts supporting 12 drug launches over the next three years, contributing to earnings growth and mid-term diversification.

Fig 4

Valuation

During the investor day, we already moved the RP valuation closer to our 2026 estimates. Today, we make no change to our numbers. We still imply a portfolio receipts growth of 9% (while the company aims to achieve 11-14% growth). On the EPS, we see a correct forecast of 10% growth (while the company implied an annual EPS growth rate of approximately 12% until 2030). This is very much supported by RP Management incorporation and a lower cost structure. Therefore, our 2026 net profit is set at €2.8 billion.

Compared to last time, the company repurchased shares and now includes 577.24 million shares outstanding. So our EPS reached $4.85.

With no change to our valuation methodology, we applied a 10x P/E target, increasing our valuation from $47 to $48.5 per share. This still implied a 35% discount vs its reference pharma sector. The company's target P/E is also aligned with its five-year historical average. The business fundamentals remain strong, and we see no reason for the sector discount. Larger transactions and earnings diversification are likely to sustain RP stock market outperformance in the near term.

Risks

RP downside risks include the Vertex franchise development and lower portfolio receipts growth (with worse-than-expected performance of current investments). This is due to increased competition and the entry of incumbent players into the market. The company is subject to interest rate risk given its debt; however, if rates rise, it is well-positioned for a better deal environment. Other risks include lower synergies from integrating the RP Management team and from value-destroying deals.

Conclusion

The Investor Day provided a tailwind for RP; however, the company's strong execution and track record are proving that it has an extended and reliable plan. With a double-digit ROE, an ongoing buyback, and many catalysts ahead, we remain buyers.
